intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah is a local developer CLI that generates coding-agent configuration for Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI from a single Go source of truth, and ships a built-in security-and-quality toolset alongside it. Its central declared promise is that every generated agent configuration carries the same set of protective hooks — linter, command guard for catastrophic shell commands, secret scanner, comment checker, and session logger — together with a built-in MCP server and a durable, structured, timestamped audit log. Authorized counterparties are the local developer, the configured agent's LLM provider, the `context7` and `pulumi` MCP servers, the built-in yaah MCP server, and the coding-agent plugin marketplace; third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good version, remote MCP MUST use TLS, MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT carry instruction-like language, and hardcoded credentials MUST be detected before a change lands.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: yaah is a Go 1.25 cobra CLI organized as `Interface → Registry → Generator`: registries collect hooks, MCP/LSP providers, skills, agents, commands, and plugins, and per-agent generators (`pkg/generator/claude.go`, `opencode.go`, `codex.go`, `copilot.go`) emit each agent's native config. Five hook handlers live in `pkg/hooks/handlers/` (`linter.go`, `guard.go`, `secretscan.go`, `commentcheck.go`, `sessionlog.go`); the command guard is a small regex denylist (`rm -rf /`, `git push --force`, hard reset, DROP, `mkfs`, `dd if=…of=/dev/`) and the secret scanner is a regex pattern set scoped to `Edit|Write|MultiEdit`. The built-in MCP server (`pkg/mcpserver/server.go`, `tools.go`) exposes `yaah_scan_secrets`, `yaah_lint`, `yaah_check_command`, `yaah_doctor`, `yaah_session_info`, `yaah_planning_status`, `yaah_planning_init` over stdio. The `.mcp.json` Praxen sees at the workspace root declares three servers — `context7` (`npx -y @context7/mcp`, unpinned), `pulumi` (`https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp`, TLS), and `yaah` (stdio `yaah serve`); the cross-agent event mapping in `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` is the determining surface for which protections actually reach each agent.

behavior_summary: The single most important pattern is **uniform protections promised, non-uniform protections shipped**: `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` declares no `PreToolUse` or `PostToolUse` mapping for the Codex CLI target, and `pkg/generator/codex.go:GenerateHooks` skips any event whose Codex name is empty — so a developer who runs `yaah generate --agent codex` receives a `.codex/hooks.json` wired only for `SessionStart`/`Stop`, with the command guard, secret scanner, linter, and comment checker silently absent from that agent's runtime path. The same fragility appears one layer deeper in `npx -y @context7/mcp` in `pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go` (and `@notionhq/notion-mcp-server` in `notion.go`): the Worker Remit says third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, but the configured invocation auto-installs the latest publish from npm on every run with no integrity check. A third structural gap — the remit's "durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications" — is half-met: `pkg/harness/harness.go:HandleHookEvent` does record tool calls and blocks into a per-session JSON store, but `pkg/hooks/handlers/sessionlog.go` only logs `SessionStart`/`SessionEnd`/`Stop` events to a plaintext line in `.claude/logs/sessions.log`, so on the Codex target (where the JSON store is never populated for tool calls) the durable audit trail required by the remit does not exist.

rules.rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS.

MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.
Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.
Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.
The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.
The MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files MUST stay consistent with each other.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

findings.summary: Codex-target generated configuration ships without command guard, secret scanner, linter, or comment checker because no PreToolUse / PostToolUse mapping exists.
findings.description: The Worker Remit's central promise is that every generated agent configuration carries the same protective hooks. `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` defines the Claude `PreToolUse` and `PostToolUse` events without any Codex equivalent, and `pkg/generator/codex.go:GenerateHooks` explicitly skips events whose `CodexEventName` is empty. The Codex output therefore contains only `SessionStart` and `Stop` hooks — a developer running `yaah generate --agent codex` receives an agent with no command guard against catastrophic shell, no secret scanner gating writes, and no human approval checkpoint on tool calls, even though the same harness wires all four on Claude and OpenCode.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger. / Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted. / High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

evidence.snippet: hookMappings table has no Codex entry for HookPreToolUse or HookPostToolUse — only SessionStart, Stop, notify are mapped for Codex.

GenerateHooks loops events and skips any whose CodexEventName is empty ("if codexEvent == \"\" || codexEvent == \"notify\" { continue }"), so PreToolUse/PostToolUse handlers are never reached on Codex.

recommended_actions: In `pkg/generator/hookmap.go`, add the Codex mappings for `HookPreToolUse` and `HookPostToolUse` (Codex CLI does expose these hook events) so the command guard and secret scanner reach the Codex runtime path.

Add a generator-level invariant test that asserts each target's generated hook config carries `command-guard` on the equivalent of `PreToolUse` and `secret-scanner` on `PostToolUse`, failing the build if any target drops a declared protective hook.

findings.summary: Third-party MCP servers (context7, notion) are launched with `npx -y` and no version pin, contradicting the remit's pinning rule.
findings.description: The Known Good Baseline section of the Worker Remit states that third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version. `pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go` ships the Context7 provider as `npx -y @context7/mcp` (default-enabled in `AllDefaults()`), and the opt-in Notion provider does the same for `@notionhq/notion-mcp-server`. Each run installs whatever current npm publish exists with no SHA, version, or signature check — the canonical agentic supply-chain rug-pull surface, exposing the configured agent to instruction-laden tool descriptions that would land in the model's context unreviewed.

tags.label: Tool definitions are signed and version-pinned

findings.policy_rule_text: Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: Server() returns Command "npx" with Args []string{"-y", "@context7/mcp"} — no @version suffix, no integrity manifest, auto-installs latest on every run.
Server() returns Command "npx" with Args []string{"-y", "@notionhq/notion-mcp-server"} — same unpinned pattern as context7.

recommended_actions: Pin both providers to a known-good npm version (e.g. `@context7/mcp@1.2.3`) and document the version-bump review process; better, fetch the package once into a checked-in lockfile or vendor directory and invoke from there.

Add a startup integrity check in `pkg/mcp/registry.go` that refuses to register an MCP provider whose `Command` is `npx` with `-y` and no `@<version>` suffix, with an explicit override flag for development.

findings.summary: No adversarial testing, threat model, or security-disclosure document exists for the protections the harness ships.
findings.description: The remit's Escalation section calls out SHOULD-level adversarial testing of the secret scanner, the command guard, session-file write protection, and the generated configurations' actual protection coverage. The repository has 14 `_test.go` files but none exercise these as attack surfaces — there is no planted-secret regression, no catastrophic-command bypass test (`rm --recursive --force /` vs the seven-pattern denylist), no MCP tool-poisoning test, no generator invariant that asserts target parity. No `SECURITY.md`, no threat model, no documented disclosure path is present in the workspace.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

evidence.snippet: Existing tests cover the happy-path Execute() return values but no test in the package fires a known-evasion command at the guard or a known-pattern-evading secret string at the scanner.
File does not exist in the workspace root — no security-disclosure or threat-model document is committed.

recommended_actions: Add a `SECURITY.md` with a disclosure mailbox and a brief threat model, and commit a `pkg/hooks/handlers/adversarial_test.go` that fires a curated list of known-evasion commands at the guard (`rm --recursive --force /`, `rm -fr /`, `find / -delete`, `\rm -rf /`) and known-pattern secrets at the scanner.
Add a generator-level invariant test that asserts each of the four targets' generated configs contains the declared protective hooks on the appropriate events.

findings.summary: Command guard is a seven-pattern regex denylist trivially bypassable by long-form flag variants of the same destructive commands.
findings.description: `NewCommandGuard()` in `pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go` installs seven case-insensitive patterns: `rm\s+-rf\s+/`, `git\s+push\s+--force\s+(origin\s+)?(main|master)`, `git\s+reset\s+--hard`, `DROP\s+(TABLE|DATABASE)`, `:\s*>\s*/`, `mkfs\.`, `dd\s+if=.*of=/dev/`. Long-form equivalents (`rm --recursive --force /`, `rm -fr /`, `find / -delete`), prefix-escape variants (`\rm -rf /`), and pipe-driven evasions (`echo y | rm -rf /` already passes — but `find / -delete` is not in the list at all) are not caught. The remit's MUST-block list reads broader than the implemented regex set, so the guard fails closed on the canonical form and silently fails open on the variants.
findings.policy_rule_text: Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.

evidence.snippet: NewCommandGuard() registers exactly seven literal regex patterns (rm -rf /, git push --force main|master, git reset --hard, DROP TABLE|DATABASE, ':>/', mkfs., dd if=…of=/dev/) — long-form flag variants and `find / -delete` are not included.

recommended_actions: Expand the denylist in `NewCommandGuard()` to cover long-form variants (`rm\s+--recursive\s+--force`, `rm\s+-fr`, `find\s+/\s+-delete`, force-push to `(main|master|trunk|develop)`) and add a test fixture file that codifies the bypasses the team has chosen to accept versus block.

findings.summary: SessionLogger handler records only SessionStart/SessionEnd/Stop to a plaintext log — the remit's "tool calls and file modifications" coverage relies entirely on the JSON session store.
findings.description: The remit requires a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications detailed enough to reconstruct session activity. `pkg/hooks/handlers/sessionlog.go:Execute` writes only one plaintext line per `SessionStart`, `SessionEnd`, or `Stop` event to `.claude/logs/sessions.log` — no tool calls, no blocks. The actual tool-call record exists only because `pkg/harness/harness.go:HandleHookEvent` independently writes to the per-session JSON store; on the Codex target (no PreToolUse/PostToolUse mapping — see PRAX-2026-05-26-001), that store is never populated for tool activity, so the remit-required record does not exist for Codex sessions.
findings.policy_rule_text: The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.

evidence.snippet: Events() returns only HookSessionStart, HookSessionEnd, HookStop; Execute() writes a single timestamped line per event — no tool-call or file-modification capture in the handler itself.

HandleHookEvent records ToolCallRecord into the session store, but this path is reached only when the harness is invoked via `yaah hook <event>` — which on Codex fires only for SessionStart/Stop, not tool use.

recommended_actions: Extend `SessionLogger.Events()` to include `HookPreToolUse` and `HookPostToolUse`, or add a dedicated `ToolCallLogger` handler that writes a structured JSON line per tool call to a durable log file independent of the session JSON store.

findings.summary: The harness configures third-party MCP servers (context7, pulumi, notion) but performs no inspection of their tool descriptions for instruction-like language.
findings.description: Rule R-04 commits the harness to ensuring MCP tool descriptions contain no instruction-like language — "neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures." The built-in side is satisfied: the seven tool descriptions in `pkg/mcpserver/tools.go` are plain action-only strings. For the third-party side there is no inspection step at all — no code path in `pkg/mcp/` enumerates a configured remote server's published tool descriptions and checks them against an instruction-language pattern, so a poisoned upstream description would land unchallenged in the model's context on the next session.

tags.label: Tool descriptions contain no hidden model instructions

findings.policy_rule_text: MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.

evidence.snippet: Registry.Register and ToConfig surface only the server config (command, args, url, env); no listTools / description-inspection call site exists anywhere under pkg/mcp/.

recommended_actions: Add a `yaah doctor --inspect-mcp` mode (or a CI-time step) that performs an MCP `tools/list` against each configured server and runs the descriptions through a small instruction-like-language detector, surfacing matches for operator review.

findings.summary: Root `AGENTS.md` is declared agent-managed and is writable without approval — a structural session-loaded-file poisoning surface absent a confirmed injection path.
findings.description: The root `AGENTS.md` carries `<!-- Managed by agent: keep sections and order; edit content, not structure -->` and is loaded as a system prompt by Claude Code via the `CLAUDE.md` symlink. The remit's rule R-06 forbids agent-managed session-loaded files being writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed. yaah's protections do gate writes: the secret scanner and comment checker run on PostToolUse Edit/Write. But neither catches an instruction-language injection into `AGENTS.md`, and no approval gate exists between an agent-driven edit and the file landing on disk — so a future-session poisoning is structurally possible. No confirmed injection path was identified in yaah itself, so this is filed at Medium per the SKILL Step 4b Part 3 scale.
findings.policy_rule_text: Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.

evidence.snippet: HTML comment "Managed by agent: keep sections and order; edit content, not structure" — declares the file agent-writable as part of normal operation; CLAUDE.md is a symlink to AGENTS.md so the same file is consumed at Claude session start.

recommended_actions: Add an `AGENTS.md`-targeted approval gate or a content-diff review hook so agent-driven edits to session-loaded files surface for operator confirmation before they land, and consider git-tracking the file with a pre-commit reviewer to keep changes auditable.

findings.summary: Secret scanner skips lock files via the `.lock` binary-extension allowlist, masking secrets accidentally pasted into them.
findings.description: `pkg/hooks/handlers/secretscan.go` declares `.lock: true` in `binaryExts` (line 159), which causes `isLikelyBinary()` to skip any file ending in `.lock` — `package-lock.json`, `yarn.lock`, `poetry.lock`, `Pipfile.lock`. These are textual JSON or TOML, not binary, and have been a documented exfiltration target for embedded auth tokens (npm `_authToken` lines, registry credentials in lockfile resolved URLs). The remit's R-05 says hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected; the scanner silently exempts an entire textual file class.
findings.policy_rule_text: Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.

evidence.snippet: binaryExts map includes ".lock": true alongside binary formats (.png, .pdf, .so, .dylib); isLikelyBinary returns true for any .lock extension, causing ScanFile to return (nil, nil) before any pattern runs.

recommended_actions: Remove `.lock` from `binaryExts` (or move it to a separate "skip-for-performance" allowlist that the user can opt back in to), and add a regression test covering an `_authToken` line in a `package-lock.json` fixture.

positives.title: All Go dependencies and CI actions pinned
positives.description: `go.mod`/`go.sum` pin the yaah dependency tree to specific versions, and every `uses:` in `.github/workflows/ci.yaml` is pinned to a full commit SHA with a version comment — supply-chain hygiene that matches the remit's R-12.

positives.title: Cosign-signed releases with SBOM
positives.description: GoReleaser is configured to sign release archives with cosign and emit SPDX SBOMs from Syft, so downstream installs have a verifiable integrity baseline.

positives.title: Closed Known Good Baseline of built-in MCP tools
positives.description: The built-in MCP server registers exactly seven tools (`yaah_scan_secrets`, `yaah_lint`, `yaah_check_command`, `yaah_doctor`, `yaah_session_info`, `yaah_planning_status`, `yaah_planning_init`) — matching the remit's authorized list — and only `yaah_planning_init` writes to the workspace.

positives.title: Structured per-session JSON store
positives.description: `HandleHookEvent` writes a structured `Session` record per session ID with timestamped `ToolCallRecord` entries, blocked-call entries, and a files-modified list — action-level granularity that supports incident reconstruction on Claude and OpenCode targets.

positives.title: Built-in MCP tool descriptions are instruction-free
positives.description: The seven yaah MCP tool descriptions are plain action statements ("Scan a file for hardcoded secrets and credentials", "Check whether a shell command is safe to run") with no "When using this tool, also…" language — the tool-poisoning indicator from KB_MCP_SECURITY is absent on the built-in side.

log_files.no_logs_note: No populated log files were present in the scan workspace — `.claude/logs/sessions.log` and the `.claude/sessions/` JSON store are created at runtime by the harness on the developer's machine; the scan input directory was source code only and contains neither. The structural monitoring gap on the Codex target is documented in finding PRAX-2026-05-26-005.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. yaah ships real, runnable safety primitives — a regex command guard, a per-Edit secret scanner, a structured session store, signed releases with cosign and SBOMs, fully pinned Go dependencies and CI action SHAs — and its central architecture (one binary, one harness, per-agent generators) is coherent and maintained. The posture is held back, not invalidated, by the central remit promise (uniform protections across every generated agent) failing structurally on the Codex target, by third-party MCP servers being shipped on `npx -y` against the remit's explicit pinning rule, and by the absence of any threat model, security disclosure process, or adversarial test evidence — the "SHOULD" item in the remit. Zero Trust and Build an AI Red Team are the lowest-scoring categories and the right places to invest first.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The Worker Remit declares a single mission (generate coding-agent configuration plus a fixed protective toolset) and a closed Known Good Baseline of authorized MCP tools and configured servers; the code enforces that scope at the harness level — only the seven named MCP tools are registered in `pkg/mcpserver/server.go:registerTools`, and the default MCP providers are exactly the three counterparties the remit authorizes. Domain expansion is opt-in (Notion via `NotionToken`, custom MCP via `pkg/mcp/providers/custom.go`) rather than enabled by default.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: yaah does not itself ingest external content into an LLM prompt — it is a config generator and an MCP tool host — so the classic indirect-injection vectors are largely out of band; the MCP tool descriptions (`pkg/mcpserver/tools.go`) are plain, action-only strings with no instruction-like language to the model. The score is held below 4 because the agent has no review or signing of remote skill content fetched via `gitcache`, which does enter the model's context on the configured downstream agents.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The command guard (`pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go`) is a seven-pattern case-insensitive regex denylist that catches `rm -rf /` but not trivially-equivalent forms (`rm --recursive --force /`, `rm -fr /`, `find / -delete`), and the secret scanner runs only on PostToolUse for `Edit|Write|MultiEdit`. More structurally, `pkg/generator/hookmap.go` has no `PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse` mapping for the Codex target, so a Codex-generated config carries neither the command guard nor the secret scanner — the remit's uniform-protection promise fails at the code level for one of the four declared targets.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: yaah's own Go dependencies are pinned in `go.mod`/`go.sum`, CI actions are pinned by SHA in `.github/workflows/ci.yaml`, and releases are signed with cosign and ship an SBOM via GoReleaser — strong for the harness binary itself. The score is held at 2 because the configured third-party MCP servers it ships by default — `context7` (`npx -y @context7/mcp` in `pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go`) and the optional `notion` (`npx -y @notionhq/notion-mcp-server`) — are unpinned `npx -y` invocations that auto-install the current npm publish on every run with no integrity check, directly contradicting the remit's "MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version" rule.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The repo has 14 `_test.go` files exercising hooks, generators, and registries, but none of them are adversarial — no planted-secret regression test for the scanner, no catastrophic-command bypass test for the guard, no MCP tool-poisoning test, no test that confirms a generated agent configuration actually carries the protections it claims. The remit's "SHOULD publish a threat model … and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections" is unmet: no `SECURITY.md`, no threat-model doc, no postmortem or red-team artifact in the repository.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: `pkg/harness/harness.go:HandleHookEvent` writes a structured per-session JSON record (`pkg/session/store.go`) capturing tool calls, blocked calls, and files modified — genuinely action-level granularity on the Claude and OpenCode targets where `PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse` are wired. The score is held at 2 because (a) the dedicated `SessionLogger` hook handler only writes `SessionStart`/`SessionEnd`/`Stop` lines to a plaintext log, and (b) on Codex no tool-call records are produced at all (no PreToolUse/PostToolUse mapping), so the "durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls" required by the remit does not exist for Codex sessions.
